Factorial Analysis of IPIP Big Five Personality Data

The Five-Factor Model of Personality (Big Five)

The Five-Factor Model (FFM), commonly referred to as the “Big Five” or the Five Factor Model (FFM) is the prevailing empirical taxonomy in contemporary personality psychology. Psychometric research have demonstrated that human personality traits can be comprehensively organized into five broad dimensions. Consequently, each individual can be mathematically represented as a single point within this 5-dimensional continuous space, where their specific coordinates correspond to their standing on each respective trait.

These five latent constructs are:

  • Extraversion: The tendency to seek stimulation, be outgoing, and engage energetically with the external world.
  • Neuroticism (or Emotional Instability): The tendency to experience negative emotions, such as anxiety, anger, or depression, and to be highly responsive to stress.
  • Agreeableness: The tendency to be compassionate, cooperative, and empathetic rather than suspicious or antagonistic towards others.
  • Conscientiousness: The tendency to display self-discipline, act dutifully, and strive for achievement against measures or outside expectations.
  • Openness to Experience: The tendency to appreciate art, emotion, adventure, unusual ideas, and variety of experience.

In this analysis, we utilize data from the International Personality Item Pool (IPIP), specifically a 50-item questionnaire designed to measure these five distinct domains. The dataset analyzed here includes responses from around 700 000 participants. Because these psychological constructs are latent—meaning they cannot be observed or measured directly—we must infer their presence and magnitude from the observable, self-reported responses to the questionnaire items. To achieve this, we rely on the mathematical framework of Exploratory Factor Analysis.1

The Mathematical Framework of Exploratory Factor Analysis (EFA)

Exploratory Factor Analysis (EFA) is a multivariate statistical technique utilized to uncover the underlying latent structure of a relatively large set of variables. Unlike Principal Component Analysis (PCA), which is merely a data reduction technique aimed at maximizing explained variance, EFA is a formal causal modeling technique. It assumes that the observed correlations between variables are the result of their shared reliance on a smaller number of unobserved continuous latent variables (the factors). As a first approximation one can see EFA as PCA’s close cousin. In fact it has been shown empirically that results from the PCA and the EFA are often very similar when the communality is sufficiently high.

Let X1,X2,…,Xp be our p observed variables, which in this context correspond to the p=50 individual questions of the survey. We postulate that these variables are standardized (mean zero and variance one). The common factor model asserts that each observed response Xi can be expressed as a linear combination of m underlying latent factors F1,F2,…,Fm (where m<p), plus a unique, unobserved error term ϵi. Variable by variable, the model is formulated as:

Xi=li1F1+li2F2+⋯+limFm+ϵifori=1,…,p

Here, lij represents the factor loading, which quantifies the direct causal effect (or weight) of the j-th latent factor on the i-th question.

Equivalently, letting X=[X1,X2,…,Xp]T be the random vector of the standardized observed responses, the model can be expressed in its more compact matrix form:

X=LF+ϵ

Where:

  • L is the p×m matrix of factor loadings (lij).
  • F=[F1,F2,…,Fm]T contains the common factors, which are assumed to have a mean of zero, a variance of one and a correlation matrix Φ.2
  • ϵ=[ϵ1,ϵ2,…,ϵp]T contains the unique factors (comprising both specific variance and measurement error). These are assumed to have a mean of zero and a covariance matrix U2.

A critical assumption of the EFA model is that the unique factors are uncorrelated with each other and with the common factors. Therefore, the uniqueness matrix U2 is a diagonal matrix. Under these assumptions, the correlation matrix of the observed variables, denoted as R, can be decomposed into two distinct components:

R=LΦLT+U2

Here, LΦLT represents the variance shared among the questions (the communality), and U2 represents the variance unique to each specific question. The fundamental objective of EFA is to estimate the matrices L and U2 given the sample correlation or covariance matrix of X.

Imports

library(psych)       # factor analysis
library(ggplot2)     # data visualisation
library(pheatmap)    # heatmaps
library(tidyr)       # data reshaping
library(dplyr)       # data manipulation
library(gridExtra)   # combining multiple plots

options(digits = 5)

Data Cleaning

data_raw <- read.csv("./IPIP-FFM-data-8Nov2018/data-final.csv", sep = "\t", na.strings = c("", " ", "NaN", "NA","NULL"))
# Select columns to only keep the 50 items that measure the 5 personality traits.
# See codebook.txt for more details on all the columns.
data <- data_raw[, 1:50]

# Remove rows where IPC != 1, as these could be multiple submissions of the same person.
# The total number of rows in the original dataset is 1 015 341, and 696 845 after cleaning.
cat("Number of lines removed to avoid multiple submission:", sum(data_raw$IPC != 1), "\n")
Number of lines removed to avoid multiple submission: 318496 
data <- data[data_raw$IPC == 1, ]

# Remove rows with missing data
n_before <- nrow(data)
data <- data[complete.cases(data), ]
n_after  <- nrow(data)
cat("Number of lines removed to avoid missing data:", n_before - n_after, "\n")
Number of lines removed to avoid missing data: 1141 
cat("Number of lines :", n_after)
Number of lines : 695704
data_mat <- as.matrix(data)
head(data)
  EXT1 EXT2 EXT3 EXT4 EXT5 EXT6 EXT7 EXT8 EXT9 EXT10 EST1 EST2 EST3 EST4 EST5
1    4    1    5    2    5    1    5    2    4     1    1    4    4    2    2
2    3    5    3    4    3    3    2    5    1     5    2    3    4    1    3
3    2    3    4    4    3    2    1    3    2     5    4    4    4    2    2
4    2    2    2    3    4    2    2    4    1     4    3    3    3    2    3

  EST6 EST7 EST8 EST9 EST10 AGR1 AGR2 AGR3 AGR4 AGR5 AGR6 AGR7 AGR8 AGR9 AGR10
1    2    2    2    3     2    2    5    2    4    2    3    2    4    3     4
2    1    2    1    3     1    1    4    1    5    1    5    3    4    5     3
3    2    2    2    1     3    1    4    1    4    2    4    1    4    4     3
4    2    2    2    4     3    2    4    3    4    2    4    2    4    3     4
6    1    2    1    2     2    2    3    1    4    2    3    2    3    4     4
7    2    2    2    4     4    1    2    1    5    3    5    3    4    4     5
  CSN1 CSN2 CSN3 CSN4 CSN5 CSN6 CSN7 CSN8 CSN9 CSN10 OPN1 OPN2 OPN3 OPN4 OPN5
1    3    4    3    2    2    4    4    2    4     4    5    1    4    1    4
2    3    2    5    3    3    1    3    3    5     3    1    2    4    2    3
3    4    2    2    2    3    3    4    2    4     2    5    1    2    1    4
4    2    4    4    4    1    2    2    3    1     4    4    2    5    2    3
6    3    2    4    1    3    2    4    3    4     3    5    1    5    1    3
7    3    2    4    2    1    4    4    2    2     5    5    2    4    3    4
  OPN6 OPN7 OPN8 OPN9 OPN10
1    1    5    3    4     5
2    1    4    2    5     3
3    2    5    3    4     4
4    1    4    4    3     3
6    1    5    4    5     2
7    1    5    5    4     4
# Computing polychoric correlations on large datasets is computationally heavy.
# We check if the matrix has already been saved to avoid recalculating it every time.

pmat_path <- "polychoric_matrix_IPIP-50.rds"

if (file.exists(pmat_path)) {
  cat("Loading saved polychoric matrix...\n")
  poly_mat <- readRDS(pmat_path)
} else {
  cat("Computing polychoric matrix (this may take a while)...\n")
  poly_mat_obj <- polychoric(data_mat, ML=FALSE) #ML=FALSE (default value) to use Olsson's two step procedure 
  poly_mat <- poly_mat_obj$rho
  
  # Save the matrix to a file for future runs
  saveRDS(poly_mat, pmat_path)
  cat("Polychoric matrix saved to", pmat_path, "\n")
}
Loading saved polychoric matrix...

Factor Analysis

Rotation and Interfactorial Correlations

We apply factor analysis to the data, to extract the Big Five personality traits, with varimax rotation to make the traits more interpretable.

We could have used non-orthogonal rotation methods, like oblimin, which allow for correlated factors. It is commonly recommended to use oblique rotation in psychometric analysis, as said in Fabrigar et al. (https://doi.org/10.1037/1082-989X.4.3.272):

For many constructs examined in psychology (e.g., mental abilities, personality traits, attitudes), there is substantial theoretical and empirical basis for expecting these constructs (or dimensions of these constructs) to be correlated with one another. Therefore, oblique rotations provide a more accurate and realistic representation of how constructs are likely to be related to one another.

However, our goal here is not to explain the correlations between factors, but to perform clustering on the factors, and we fear that oblique rotation will deform the space in a way that will influence the clustering. Additionally, Costello and Osborne (https://doi.org/10.1037/1082-989X.4.3.272) suggest that orthogonal and oblique rotation often produce similar results, so we will not lose much interpretability by using varimax rotation. The results given in alternative B of this notebook agree with this statement.

Factor Analysis Extraction Method

psych::fa() with fm="minres" uses the minres method3 to perform factor analysis, which does not assume that factors are normally distributed, contrary to the maximum likelihood (ML) method.

Since the assumption that factors follow a Gaussian distribution directly contradicts the assumption of the Gaussian mixture model we will use later, we think it is preferable to use the ULS method instead of the ML method. However, this does not seem to bother Gerlach, M., Farb, B., Revelle, W. et al. (https://doi.org/10.1038/s41562-018-0419-z), who use the ML method before using GMM. For the sake of comparison, we also perform factor analysis with the ML method in alternative A of this notebook, and the results are very similar to those given by the ULS method.

How Many Factors to Extract?

A common method to determine the number of factors to extract is to look at the scree plot, which is a plot of the eigenvalues of the correlation matrix of the data. The eigenvalues represent the amount of variance explained by each factor. The scree plot shows a clear elbow at 5 factors, which suggests that we should extract 5 factors. This is consistent with the fact that we are trying to extract the Big Five personality traits.

# The ULS method has several names: minres, iterated principal factor method,
# or unweighted least squares method.
fa_result <- fa(poly_mat, nfactors = 5, rotate = "varimax", fm = "minres", n.obs = nrow(data_mat), scores="Anderson")

data_fa <- as.data.frame(factor.scores(data_mat, fa_result)$scores)

# Eigenvalues of the original (unreduced) correlation matrix
eigenvalues <- fa_result$values
cat("Eigenvalues:", eigenvalues, "\n")
Eigenvalues: 7.4533 5.0033 3.6164 3.2961 2.5166 1.5868 0.70334 0.45427 0.34436 0.26689 0.24318 0.22326 0.20283 0.17094 0.13199 0.12306 0.071648 0.04725 0.029906 0.0066417 0.0039356 -0.0069655 -0.028663 -0.045116 -0.046835 -0.071656 -0.080234 -0.080681 -0.097554 -0.1063 -0.11045 -0.11505 -0.12383 -0.13058 -0.13271 -0.14557 -0.15228 -0.15989 -0.17801 -0.18149 -0.19886 -0.20977 -0.21367 -0.21868 -0.24649 -0.25807 -0.28086 -0.29869 -0.3446 -0.34671 
# Plot the ten first eigenvalues in a scree plot
top_10_eigenvalues <- eigenvalues[1:10]
scree_df <- data.frame(factor = 1:10, eigenvalue = top_10_eigenvalues)

ggplot(scree_df, aes(x = factor, y = eigenvalue)) +
  geom_line(color = "blue", linewidth = 1) +
  geom_point(color = "blue", size = 3) +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = 1:10) +
  labs(title = "Scree Plot", x = "Factor Number", y = "Eigenvalue") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13) +
  theme(panel.grid.minor = element_blank())

Looking at the scree plot, it is not clear that we should extract five factors; the solution with six factors also seems plausible. We will look at Velicer’s Minimal Average Partial (MAP) criterion, which is commonly considered to be one of the best criterion for choosing how much factor we should extract.

Velicer’s MAP criterion

Before defining the MAP criterion, we first need to define the partial correlation matrix.

Let X be a centered random vector of dimension (p+m)×1. We partition X into two subvectors: X1 of dimension p×1 and X2 of dimension m×1, such that:

X=[X1X2]

Let C:=Cov(X) . Partitioning C conformably with X yields:

C=[C11C12C12TC22] where C11=Cov(X1), C22=Cov(X2), and C12=Cov(X1,X2). We assume that C22 is nonsingular.

To partial out the effect of X2 from X1, we consider the best linear predictor of X1 based on X2. This is derived from the population linear regression of X1 on X2. The matrix of regression coefficients is given by C12C22−14.

We now define the residual vector e, which represents the portion of X1 that is orthogonal to (and thus uncorrelated with) the vector space spanned by X2:

e=X1−C12C22−1X2

The matrix of partial variances and covariances of X1 given X2, denoted C11∗, is defined as the covariance matrix of the residual vector e. By applying the bilinear properties of the covariance operator, we compute Cov(e):

C11∗=Cov(X1−C12C22−1X2) C11∗=Cov(X1)+Cov(C12C22−1X2)−Cov(X1,C12C22−1X2)−Cov(C12C22−1X2,X1) Applying the property Cov(AY)=ACov(Y)A′ for a constant matrix A and random vector Y:

C11∗=C11+(C12C22−1)Cov(X2)(C12C22−1)′−Cov(X1,X2)(C12C22−1)′−(C12C22−1)Cov(X2,X1) Substitute the block matrices C22=Cov(X2) and C12=Cov(X1,X2). Note that Cov(X2,X1)=C12′ and (C22−1)′=C22−1 due to symmetry:

C11∗=C11+C12C22−1C22C22−1C12′−C12C22−1C12′−C12C22−1C12′ Since C22C22−1=I, the expression simplifies to:

C11∗=C11+C12C22−1C12′−2C12C22−1C12′ C11∗=C11−C12C22−1C12′

A correlation matrix is derived by scaling a covariance matrix by the inverse of the standard deviations of its variables. Let D be a diagonal matrix containing the main diagonal elements of the partial covariance matrix C11∗:

D=Diag(C11∗)The diagonal elements of D are the partial variances. Consequently, D−1/2 is a diagonal matrix containing the reciprocals of the partial standard deviations. Pre-multiplying and post-multiplying C11∗ by this matrix standardizes the partial covariances, yielding the partial correlation matrix R11∗:

R11∗=D−1/2C11∗D−1/2Substituting the derived expression for C11∗, we obtain R11∗=D−1/2(C11−C12C22−1C12′)D−1/2.

To connect the general partial correlation framework to the EFA model, the notations translate as follows:

  • X1≡X: The vector of observed variables.

  • X2≡F: The vector of latent factors (or extracted principal components).

  • C11∗≡U2: The residual covariance matrix corresponds to the diagonal uniqueness matrix.

  • R11∗≡I (Theoretically): The partial correlation matrix of the observed variables after partialling out the factors. As the standardized version of the diagonal U2 matrix, a perfectly specified theoretical model yields the identity matrix.

The Minimum Average Partial (MAP) criterion is just the sum of squared off diagonal elements of the empirical partial correlation matrix R^11∗, denoted here by fm. It is supposed to be minimal when the number of extracted factors m attains its optimal values.

Here is another explanation of the rational behind this criterion from Velicer’s original 1976 paper:

In order to better understand why the value fm first decreases and then increases, consider the general form of a partial correlation equation rij,y=rij−rjyriy((1−rjy2)(1−riy2))1/2 where i,j represent any two of the p observed variables and y represents a [factor]. With no loss of generality, we assume rij,rij, and riy to be positive. The value of the partial correlation will decrease as long as the numerator decreases faster than the denominator. The value of the partial correlation will increase whenever the reverse is true; for example, when riy is large and rjy is small. Such a situation would occur if a [factor] has a high correlation with only one variable and near zero correlations with the others (commonly referred to as a “specific”). This explanation can be extended inductively to the case where more than one [factor] has been extracted by using k-th order partials calculated from ( k−1-th) order partials. Using this stopping rule, the first m [factors] could be referred to as “common [factors]”. We would expect that two or more variables would have “high” loadings on these [factors]. The remaining m+1 to p [factors] may include some that have adequate reliability. However, these will be “unique” [factors] (i.e., having “high” loadings on only one of the original variables).

In other terms, as you extract “common factors” (which relate to multiple variables), the remaining correlations between those variables successfully decrease. But if you extract a “unique factor” (which relates strongly to only one variable), the mathematical formula shrinks the denominator more than the numerator, which artificially inflates the remaining correlations. Therefore, the overall value fm drops while you are finding useful common factors, and starts to rise as soon as you begin extracting factors specific to single variables.

Very Simple Structure criterion

Most people, when interpreting a factor solution, will highlight the large (salient) loadings and ignore the small loadings. That is, they are interpreting the factor matrix as if it had simple structure. How well does this simplified matrix reproduce the original matrix ? To quantify this, let c be the complexity (number of non zero loadings) of an item and maxc be the greatest (absolute) c loadings for an item, then find the Very Simple Structure matrix, S^c, where s^cij={l^ijif l^ij∈maxc(l^i.)0otherwiseThen let
R^sc∗=R^−S^cS^cTand
VSSc=1−1R^sc∗⊙21′−tr(R^sc∗)1R^⊙21′−tr(R^)The VSS criterion for a complexity c is 1 - the squared residual correlations divided by the squared observed correlations, where the residuals are found by the “simplified” factor equation R^sc∗=R^−S^cS^cT. 5

The VSS criterion is less used than the MAP in the literature. In fact the VSS criterion makes the strong assumption that the loadings of variable on factors are simple, which is not always the case. Our personal preference goes to the MAP criterion, which seems more objective.

Here are now the results of both MAP and VSS criterion.

# Run MAP and VSS test on the polychoric correlation matrix
n_of_factors_diagnostic <- VSS(poly_mat, n.obs = nrow(data_mat), n = 10, plot = FALSE)
summary(n_of_factors_diagnostic)

Very Simple Structure
VSS complexity 1 achieves a maximimum of 0.65  with  6  factors
VSS complexity 2 achieves a maximimum of 0.8  with  5  factors

The Velicer MAP criterion achieves a minimum of 0.03  with  6  factors

The MAP criterion advises us to extract six factors. In fact six factor model have largely been discussed in the literature, such as the HEXACO, which is more or less the Big 5 with the addition of a honesty-humility trait. However, since our data have been specifically designed to estimate the Big 5 model, we will stick to a five factor model here.

# Plot the weights of the 50 items on the 5 personality traits as a heatmap.
# NOTE: The factor order below is inferred by inspecting which group of items
# loads most strongly on each factor (see the loading heatmap).
trait_names <- c("Extraversion", "Neuroticism", "Agreeableness",
                 "Conscientiousness", "Openness")

data_fa  <- data_fa[, c(2,1,3,5,4)]
fa_result$loadings <- fa_result$loadings[, c(2,1,3,5,4)]

# fa$loadings is items × factors; transpose to get traits × items
weights <- t(as.matrix(fa_result$loadings))


rownames(weights) <- trait_names

coolwarm <- colorRampPalette(c("#3B4CC0", "white", "#B40426"))(200)
lim      <- max(abs(weights))
breaks   <- seq(-lim, lim, length.out = 201)

pheatmap(
  weights,
  color        = coolwarm,
  breaks       = breaks,
  cluster_rows = FALSE,
  cluster_cols = FALSE,
  main         = "Weights of the 50 items on the 5 personality traits",
  fontsize      = 8,
  fontsize_row  = 9
)
# From the previous plot, we can infer that factors are ordered as listed in trait_names.
# Assign the Big Five trait names as column names.
colnames(data_fa) <- trait_names


data_fa_long <- pivot_longer(data_fa, cols = everything(),
                             names_to = "trait", values_to = "score")
data_fa_long$trait <- factor(data_fa_long$trait)

ggplot(data_fa_long, aes(x = score)) +
  geom_histogram(aes(y = after_stat(density)), bins = 60, alpha = 0.6, color = NA) +
  geom_density(color = "darkred", linewidth = 0.8) +
  facet_wrap(~trait, nrow = 1, scales = "free") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 11) +
  labs(x = "Score", y = "Density")
write.csv(data_fa, "IPIP-50_FFM_factor_scores.csv", row.names = FALSE)

Alternative A: Comparison of ULS vs ML Extraction Methods for Factor Analysis

# Perform factor analysis with the maximum likelihood (ML) method,
# for comparison with the ULS method.
fa_ml     <- fa(poly_mat, nfactors = 5, rotate = "varimax", fm = "ml", n.obs = nrow(data_mat))
data_fa_ml <- as.data.frame(factor.scores(data_mat, fa_ml)$scores)

# Exchange column to properly match with traits
data_fa_ml  <- data_fa_ml[, c(1, 2, 3, 5, 4)]
fa_ml$loadings <- fa_ml$loadings[, c(1, 2, 3, 5, 4)]

colnames(data_fa_ml) <- trait_names
loadings_ml <- as.matrix(fa_ml$loadings)

weights_ml <- t(loadings_ml)

# Loading matrix comparison plots
breaks_full <- seq(-1, 1, length.out = 201)

p1 <- pheatmap(weights_ml, color = coolwarm, breaks = breaks_full,
               cluster_rows = FALSE, cluster_cols = FALSE,
               main = "Factor loading (ML Method)",
               fontsize = 7, fontsize_row = 8, silent = TRUE)
p2 <- pheatmap(weights,    color = coolwarm, breaks = breaks_full,
               cluster_rows = FALSE, cluster_cols = FALSE,
               main = "Factor loading (ULS Method)",
               fontsize = 7, fontsize_row = 8, silent = TRUE)
diff_ml <- unclass(weights)- unclass(weights_ml) #unclass to avoid the rounding of "loadings" class
p3 <- pheatmap(diff_ml,    color = coolwarm, breaks = breaks_full,
               cluster_rows = FALSE, cluster_cols = FALSE,
               main = "Difference (ML - ULS)",
               fontsize = 7, fontsize_row = 8, silent = TRUE)

grid.arrange(p1$gtable, p2$gtable, p3$gtable, ncol = 3,
             widths = c(1, 1, 1))
cat("Difference between the weights of the ML method and the ULS method:\n")
Difference between the weights of the ML method and the ULS method:

Agreeableness     -0.00326076 -0.0200741 -0.00873876 -0.0216281 -0.00574855
Conscientiousness -0.00073473  0.0086189  0.00754581  0.0021255 -0.00084658
Openness          -0.00054522 -0.0070089 -0.00046508 -0.0077722 -0.01018927
                        CSN6        CSN7       CSN8       CSN9      CSN10
Extraversion       0.0160336  0.00272102  0.0102221  0.0053045  0.0075071
Neuroticism        0.0201296  0.00062705  0.0089329 -0.0004101  0.0041225
Agreeableness     -0.0210654 -0.00171419 -0.0212416 -0.0047424 -0.0047388
Conscientiousness  0.0046417  0.00088043  0.0076689 -0.0030531  0.0060856
Openness          -0.0127010  0.00112584 -0.0115639 -0.0051600 -0.0030698
                        OPN1       OPN2       OPN3       OPN4       OPN5
Extraversion       0.0024460  0.0179937  0.0062370  0.0183774  0.0118436
Neuroticism        0.0080229  0.0142297  0.0028323  0.0103226  0.0053227
Agreeableness     -0.0087366 -0.0205917 -0.0034214 -0.0255719 -0.0068723
Conscientiousness  0.0050448  0.0040837  0.0039640  0.0029642  0.0032417
Openness           0.0282043 -0.0348169 -0.0142979 -0.0265531 -0.0332200
                        OPN6       OPN7       OPN8       OPN9      OPN10
Extraversion       0.0072254  0.0083674  0.0028012  0.0047411  0.0084230
Neuroticism        0.0047008  0.0021057  0.0057278  0.0080299  0.0058342
Agreeableness     -0.0209224 -0.0142542 -0.0109625 -0.0035503 -0.0012663
Conscientiousness -0.0013243  0.0062281  0.0055354  0.0066786  0.0036745
Openness           0.0094418  0.0023395  0.0247927  0.0024562 -0.0250617
# Histogram comparison: ML vs ULS distributions for each trait

data_compare <- bind_rows(
  pivot_longer(data_fa_ml, cols = everything(),
               names_to = "trait", values_to = "score") %>%
    mutate(method = "ML"),
  pivot_longer(data_fa, cols = everything(),
               names_to = "trait", values_to = "score") %>%
    mutate(method = "ULS")
)
data_compare$trait <- factor(data_compare$trait)

ggplot(data_compare, aes(x = score, color = method, fill = method)) +
  
  geom_density(alpha = 0.4, linewidth = 0.8) +
  
  facet_wrap(~trait, nrow = 1, scales = "free") +
  
  scale_fill_manual(values  = c("ML" = "royalblue", "ULS" = "darkred")) +
  scale_color_manual(values = c("ML" = "royalblue", "ULS" = "darkred")) +
  
  theme_minimal(base_size = 11) +
  theme(legend.position = "bottom") +
  labs(x = "Score", y = "Density")

We can see that the loadings and distributions of the factors with the ULS method are very similar to those given by the factor analysis with the ML method, which suggests that the choice of extraction method does not have a strong influence on the results. However, we think it is preferable to use the ULS method, since it does not assume that factors are normally distributed, which is more consistent with the assumptions of the Gaussian mixture model we will use later.

Alternative B: Comparison of Varimax vs Oblimin Rotation Methods for Factor Analysis

In the main part of the notebook, we use orthogonal rotation (varimax) to make the factors more interpretable. However, we could have used oblique (oblimin) rotation, which allows for correlated factors. We will see that the results given by the oblimin rotation are very similar to those given by the varimax rotation, which suggests that the choice of rotation method does not have a strong influence on the results.

# Perform factor analysis with the oblimin rotation method,
# for comparison with the varimax rotation used above.
# scores="tenBerge" to preserve correlation
fa_obli      <- fa(poly_mat, nfactors = 5, rotate = "oblimin", fm = "minres", n.obs = nrow(data_mat), scores="tenBerge")
data_fa_obli <- as.data.frame(factor.scores(data_mat, fa_obli)$scores)

# Exchange column to properly match with traits
data_fa_obli  <- data_fa_obli[, c(1, 2, 3, 5, 4)]
fa_obli$loadings <- fa_obli$loadings[, c(1, 2, 3, 5, 4)]

loadings_obli  <- as.matrix(fa_obli$loadings)

# From our (not shown) observations, factors are ordered as follows:
colnames(data_fa_obli) <- trait_names

weights_obli <- t(loadings_obli)
rownames(weights_obli) <- trait_names

# Loading matrix comparison plots
p_obli <- pheatmap(weights_obli, color = coolwarm, breaks = breaks_full,
                   cluster_rows = FALSE, cluster_cols = FALSE,
                   main = "Factor loading (Oblique Rotation)",
                   fontsize = 7, fontsize_row = 8, silent = TRUE)
p_vari <- pheatmap(weights,      color = coolwarm, breaks = breaks_full,
                   cluster_rows = FALSE, cluster_cols = FALSE,
                   main = "Factor loading (Orthogonal Rotation)",
                   fontsize = 7, fontsize_row = 8, silent = TRUE)
diff_obli <- unclass(weights_obli) - unclass(weights)
p_diff <- pheatmap(diff_obli,   color = coolwarm, breaks = breaks_full,
                   cluster_rows = FALSE, cluster_cols = FALSE,
                   main = "Difference (Oblique - Orthogonal)",
                   fontsize = 7, fontsize_row = 8, silent = TRUE)

grid.arrange(p_obli$gtable, p_vari$gtable, p_diff$gtable, ncol = 3,
             widths = c(1, 1, 1))
cat("Difference between the weights of varimax and oblimin rotation:\n")
Difference between the weights of varimax and oblimin rotation:

Correlation Between the Factors Obtained with the Oblimin Rotation

Unlike the factors obtained with the varimax rotation, the factors obtained with the oblimin rotation are correlated, as expected. However, the correlations between factors are not very strong, which suggests that the factors are still relatively independent from each other. This is consistent with the fact that the results given by the oblimin rotation are very similar to those given by the varimax rotation. We give here a comparison of the correlation matrix we found with the one given by Van der Linden et al. in their 2010 meta-analysis (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jrp.2010.03.003).

# Correlation matrix of the 5 personality traits obtained with the oblique rotation
corr_obli <- fa_obli$Phi

rownames(corr_obli) <- colnames(corr_obli) <- trait_names

# (Uncorrected) correlation matrix from the meta-analysis of Van der Linden et al. (2010)
corr_meta <- matrix(c(
   1.00, -0.26,  0.18,  0.21,  0.31,
  -0.26,  1.00, -0.26, -0.32, -0.12,
   0.18, -0.26,  1.00,  0.31,  0.14,
   0.21, -0.32,  0.31,  1.00,  0.14,
   0.31, -0.12,  0.14,  0.14,  1.00
), nrow = 5, byrow = TRUE,
   dimnames = list(trait_names, trait_names))

sd_meta <- matrix(c(
  0.00, 0.11, 0.15, 0.15, 0.12,
  0.11, 0.00, 0.14, 0.18, 0.12,
  0.15, 0.14, 0.00, 0.14, 0.12,
  0.15, 0.18, 0.14, 0.00, 0.15,
  0.12, 0.12, 0.12, 0.15, 0.00
), nrow = 5, byrow = TRUE)

# Build annotation matrices
annot_obli <- matrix(sprintf("%.2f", corr_obli), nrow = 5,
                     dimnames = dimnames(corr_obli))
annot_meta <- matrix("", nrow = 5, ncol = 5, dimnames = dimnames(corr_meta))
for (i in 1:5) {
  for (j in 1:5) {
    if (i == j) {
      annot_meta[i, j] <- sprintf("%.2f", corr_meta[i, j])
    } else {
      annot_meta[i, j] <- sprintf("%.2f \n(+/-%.2f)", corr_meta[i, j], sd_meta[i, j])
    }
  }
}

# Show the two correlation matrices side by side for comparison
p_corr_obli <- pheatmap(
  corr_obli, color = coolwarm, breaks = breaks_full,
  cluster_rows = FALSE, cluster_cols = FALSE,
  display_numbers = annot_obli, number_format = "%.2f",
  main = "Correlation Matrix (Oblique Rotation)",
  fontsize = 9, silent = TRUE
)
p_corr_meta <- pheatmap(
  corr_meta, color = coolwarm, breaks = breaks_full,
  cluster_rows = FALSE, cluster_cols = FALSE,
  display_numbers = annot_meta,
  main = "Correlation Matrix (Meta-Analysis)",
  fontsize = 9, silent = TRUE
)

grid.arrange(p_corr_obli$gtable, p_corr_meta$gtable, ncol = 2,
             widths = c(1, 1))
# Histogram comparison: oblimin vs varimax distributions for each trait
data_compare_obli <- bind_rows(
  pivot_longer(data_fa_obli, cols = everything(),
               names_to = "trait", values_to = "score") %>%
    mutate(method = "oblimin (oblique)"),
  pivot_longer(data_fa, cols = everything(),
               names_to = "trait", values_to = "score") %>%
    mutate(method = "varimax (orthogonal)")
)
data_compare_obli$trait <- factor(data_compare_obli$trait)

ggplot(data_compare_obli, aes(x = score, color = method, fill = method)) +
  
  geom_density(alpha = 0.4, linewidth = 0.8) +
  
  facet_wrap(~trait, nrow = 1, scales = "free") +
  
  scale_fill_manual(values  = c("oblimin (oblique)" = "royalblue", "varimax (orthogonal)" = "darkred")) +
  scale_color_manual(values = c("oblimin (oblique)" = "royalblue", "varimax (orthogonal)" = "darkred")) +
  
  theme_minimal(base_size = 11) +
  theme(legend.position = "bottom") +
  labs(x = "Score", y = "Density")

Alternative C: Pearson vs polychoric matrix for factor extraction

Polychoric correlation matrix

The trouble with Likert scales

Exploratory factor analysis is based on a correlation matrix: from a correlation matrix, it infers the underlying factors that explain the correlations between the items. The core assumption of our study is that the responses to the questionnaire are strongly determined by some underlying continuous factors. So it seems intuitive that each person has an “internal” continuous response to each question that is a linear combination of the underlying (continuous) factors plus a random noise. This “internal” response is then discretized into an ordinal Likert scale when answering the questionnaire. In a perfect world, we would have direct access to these “internal” continuous responses, which are more reflective of the personality of the participant, and then we would perform the factor analysis on the correlations of these “internal” continuous responses. But we only have access to the Likert scales, which correlation underestimate the “true” underlying correlation, as we can see in the following theorem:

Theorem: If (Z1,Z2) have a joint bivariate normal distribution with correlation coefficient ρ, then for any Borel-measurable functions f and g such that f(Z1) and g(Z2) possess finite variances, the absolute correlation between the transformed variables is bounded by the absolute value of ρ: |corr(f(Z1),g(Z2))|≤|ρ| The equality only happens when f=g=id.

Proof: The proof rely on the fact that we can express L2 random variables (w.r.t. Gaussian measure) as series of standardized Hermite-Tchebycheff polynomial, which are orthogonal w.r.t. Gaussian measure. See H.O. Lancaster(1957) for the full proof.

Now if we assume that Z1 and Z2 are Gaussian, we can define L1 and L2 as the observed ordinal Likert variables. These discrete variables are created by partitioning the continuous variables Z1 and Z2 using a set of thresholds (e.g., L1=i if αi−1<Z1≤αi, and L2=j if βj−1<Z2≤βj). Since L1 and L2 are created through step functions of the underlying continuous variables (i.e., L1=f(Z1) and L2=g(Z2)), it rigorously follows that the correlation between the discrete responses is strictly bounded by the correlation of the “internal” continuous variables:

|corr(L1,L2)|<|ρ|

This is why Likert-scale questionnaires tend to underestimate the “true” correlation between the questions. From this example, it seems very plausible that the naive use of Pearson correlation on Likert scale can give poor results. One way of addressing this issue is to use a polychoric correlation matrix.

Polychoric correlation estimation

Given what we have said above, if we use the Pearson correlation matrix, we will obtain an estimation of the correlation of the Likert discrete variables, not the correlation of the underlying continuous ones. So we need a way to infer the correlation of the underlying continuous variables given their discretized versions. This is exactly what polychoric correlation is supposed to do.

To compute the polychoric correlation, we assume that the underlying continuous latent variables (Z1,Z2) follow a standard bivariate normal distribution with correlation ρ. The theoretical probability πij of an observation falling into the i-th category for L1 and the j-th category for L2 is the following:

πij=Φ2(αi,βj;ρ)−Φ2(αi−1,βj;ρ)−Φ2(αi,βj−1;ρ)+Φ2(αi−1,βj−1;ρ)

where Φ2 is the cumulative distribution function (CDF) of the standard bivariate normal distribution.

To estimate these parameters from an observed dataset where nij represents the frequency of responses in cell (i,j), we use Maximum Likelihood Estimation (MLE). The log-likelihood function to maximize is defined as:

ℓ(ρ,α,β)=∑i∑jnijln⁡[πij(ρ,α,β)]

Olsson’s Two-Step Method Maximizing this log-likelihood for all parameters (ρ, α, β) simultaneously is computationally expensive. To solve this, the standard approach relies on Olsson’s (1979) two-step procedure, which consists of first estimating the thresholds α and β using only the marginal distributions, and then estimating the correlation coefficient ρ. Despite the theoretical non-optimality of this procedure, the gain in computational efficiency outweighs the loss in precision, as reported in Olsson’s article. However, it is still possible to use a full maximum likelihood estimation for maximum statistical efficiency. In this code, we will use Olsson’s two step method (parameter ML=FALSE in the polychoric function from psych package).

Empirical evidence of the superiority of polychoric correlation matrix in EFA

The methodological importance of this choice is highlighted by Holgado–Tello et al (https://doi.org/10.1007/s11135-008-9190-y ). The authors demonstrate that when data are ordinal, polychoric correlations provide a more accurate and stable reproduction of the original factor structure compared to Pearson correlations, which can lead to biased loadings and incorrect factor counts.

# Perform factor analysis with the oblimin rotation method using Pearson correlations.
# NOTE: The default was set to polychoric at the beginning of the notebook for all main analyses.
fa_pearson      <- fa(data_mat, nfactors = 5, rotate = "oblimin", fm = "minres",
                   cor = "cor", scores="tenBerge")
data_fa_pearson <- as.data.frame(fa_pearson$scores)
loadings_pearson  <- as.matrix(fa_pearson$loadings)

# Apply the trait names to the columns and rows
colnames(data_fa_pearson) <- trait_names

weights_pearson <- t(loadings_pearson)
rownames(weights_pearson) <- trait_names

# Loading matrix comparison plots
p_poly <- pheatmap(weights_obli, color = coolwarm, breaks = breaks_full,
                   cluster_rows = FALSE, cluster_cols = FALSE,
                   main = "Factor loading (Polychoric Oblimin)",
                   fontsize = 7, fontsize_row = 8, silent = TRUE)

p_pearson <- pheatmap(weights_pearson, color = coolwarm, breaks = breaks_full,
                   cluster_rows = FALSE, cluster_cols = FALSE,
                   main = "Factor loading (Pearson Oblimin)",
                   fontsize = 7, fontsize_row = 8, silent = TRUE)

# Calculate difference using unclass() to prevent the "loadings" rounding issue
diff_pearson <- unclass(weights_pearson) - unclass(weights_obli)

p_diff <- pheatmap(diff_pearson,   color = coolwarm, breaks = breaks_full,
                   cluster_rows = FALSE, cluster_cols = FALSE,
                   main = "Difference (Pearson - Polychoric)",
                   fontsize = 7, fontsize_row = 8, silent = TRUE)

grid.arrange(p_poly$gtable, p_pearson$gtable, p_diff$gtable, ncol = 3,
             widths = c(1, 1, 1))
cat("Difference between the weights of Pearson and Polychoric:\n")
Difference between the weights of Pearson and Polychoric:
print(round(diff_pearson, 5))

Openness           0.00962 -0.00707 -0.01780  0.00317  0.01648 -0.00326
                      CSN7     CSN8     CSN9    CSN10     OPN1     OPN2
Extraversion       0.00129 -0.02808 -0.00821 -0.00817 -0.01315 -0.03034
Neuroticism        0.02506 -0.08967  0.02306  0.02018 -0.01208 -0.05278
Agreeableness      0.00449  0.03972  0.00033  0.00769  0.01695  0.02684
Conscientiousness -0.04957 -0.02544 -0.03040 -0.04495 -0.01635 -0.02564
Openness           0.01846  0.00634  0.01880 -0.00664 -0.03187  0.05992
                      OPN3     OPN4     OPN5     OPN6     OPN7     OPN8
Extraversion      -0.00694 -0.03856 -0.03307 -0.02425 -0.02572 -0.01408
Neuroticism       -0.04476 -0.05112 -0.00669 -0.04932  0.00583 -0.03321
Agreeableness     -0.00643  0.04173  0.02624  0.04661  0.02845  0.02137
Conscientiousness -0.00954 -0.03872 -0.02942 -0.02874 -0.03637 -0.02472
Openness          -0.05077  0.06226 -0.03274  0.05535 -0.03830 -0.02443
                      OPN9    OPN10
Extraversion       0.02112 -0.02578
Neuroticism       -0.03248 -0.02495
Agreeableness     -0.01632  0.01016
Conscientiousness -0.01032 -0.01835
Openness          -0.05250 -0.04567
# Histogram comparison: Pearson vs Polychoric correlation matrix for factor analysis for each trait

data_compare_pearson <- bind_rows(
  pivot_longer(data_fa_pearson, cols = everything(),
               names_to = "trait", values_to = "score") %>%
    mutate(method = "Pearson"),
  pivot_longer(data_fa_obli, cols = everything(),
               names_to = "trait", values_to = "score") %>%
    mutate(method = "Polychoric")
)

# Convert trait to factor to control facet order if needed
data_compare_pearson$trait <- factor(data_compare_pearson$trait)

ggplot(data_compare_pearson, aes(x = score, color = method, fill = method)) +
  
  geom_density(alpha = 0.4, linewidth = 0.8) +
  
  facet_wrap(~trait, nrow = 1, scales = "free") +
  
  scale_fill_manual(values  = c("Pearson" = "royalblue", "Polychoric" = "darkred")) +
  scale_color_manual(values = c("Pearson" = "royalblue", "Polychoric" = "darkred")) +
  
  theme_minimal(base_size = 11) +
  theme(legend.position = "bottom") +
  labs(x = "Score", y = "Density")
corr_poly <- fa_obli$Phi

corr_pearson <- fa_pearson$Phi

annot_poly <- matrix(sprintf("%.2f", corr_poly), nrow = 5)
annot_pearson <- matrix(sprintf("%.2f", corr_pearson), nrow = 5)

rownames(corr_poly) <- colnames(corr_poly) <- trait_names
rownames(corr_pearson) <- colnames(corr_pearson) <- trait_names

p_corr_poly <- pheatmap(
  corr_poly, color = coolwarm, breaks = breaks_full,
  cluster_rows = FALSE, cluster_cols = FALSE,
  display_numbers = annot_poly, number_format = "%.2f",
  main = "Factor Correlation (Polychoric Oblimin)",
  fontsize = 9, silent = TRUE
)

p_corr_pearson <- pheatmap(
  corr_pearson, color = coolwarm, breaks = breaks_full,
  cluster_rows = FALSE, cluster_cols = FALSE,
  display_numbers = annot_pearson, number_format = "%.2f",
  main = "Factor Correlation (Pearson Oblimin)",
  fontsize = 9, silent = TRUE
)

p_corr_meta_comp <- pheatmap(
  corr_meta, color = coolwarm, breaks = breaks_full,
  cluster_rows = FALSE, cluster_cols = FALSE,
  display_numbers = annot_meta,
  main = "Factor Correlation (Meta-Analysis)",
  fontsize = 9, silent = TRUE
)

grid.arrange(p_corr_poly$gtable, p_corr_pearson$gtable, p_corr_meta_comp$gtable, 
             ncol = 3, widths = c(1, 1, 1))
pearson_mat <- cor(data_mat)


diff_item_mat <- poly_mat - pearson_mat

lim_diff <- max(abs(diff_item_mat))
breaks_diff <- seq(-lim_diff, lim_diff, length.out = 201)

p_item_pearson <- pheatmap(
  pearson_mat, 
  color = coolwarm, 
  breaks = breaks_full,
  cluster_rows = FALSE, 
  cluster_cols = FALSE,
  main = "Item Correlation (Pearson)",
  show_rownames = FALSE, 
  show_colnames = FALSE, 
  silent = TRUE
)

p_item_poly <- pheatmap(
  poly_mat, 
  color = coolwarm, 
  breaks = breaks_full,
  cluster_rows = FALSE, 
  cluster_cols = FALSE,
  main = "Item Correlation (Polychoric)",
  show_rownames = FALSE, 
  show_colnames = FALSE, 
  silent = TRUE
)

p_item_diff <- pheatmap(
  diff_item_mat, 
  color = coolwarm, 
  breaks = breaks_diff,
  cluster_rows = FALSE, 
  cluster_cols = FALSE,
  main = "Difference (Polychoric - Pearson)",
  show_rownames = FALSE, 
  show_colnames = FALSE, 
  silent = TRUE
)

# Display the heatmaps side by side
grid.arrange(
  p_item_pearson$gtable, 
  p_item_poly$gtable, 
  p_item_diff$gtable, 
  ncol = 3, 
  widths = c(1, 1, 1)
)
# Print a numerical summary of the differences in the console
cat("Numerical summary of the differences (Polychoric - Pearson) between items:\n")
Numerical summary of the differences (Polychoric - Pearson) between items:
summary(as.vector(diff_item_mat))
   Min. 1st Qu.  Median    Mean 3rd Qu.    Max. 
-0.0621  0.0000  0.0119  0.0124  0.0255  0.0775

As we have said previously, the plot clearly indicates that Pearson correlation tends to systematically underestimate the true correlation between questions (not factor).

Footnotes

  1. It is sometimes more simply referred as Factor Analysis, however we prefer to be precise and distinguish Exploratory Factor Analysis from Confirmatory Factor Analysis (which will not be used here).↩︎

  2. It is sometimes assumed that the common factors are uncorrelated, so that Φ=I. In this case we get the simpler equation R=LLT+U2.↩︎

  3. The minres method aim to minimise the square of the off diagonal coefficient of difference between the sample covariance matrix S and the model-implied covariance matrix i.e: $ *{*\hat{\mathbf{L}}} ( {ij}-(^T){ij})^2$↩︎

  4. For those who are not familiar with this last expression, we can give an equivalent expression in the usual (more restrictive) multiple linear regression framework:

    Let’s assume that we have a uniform probability on the sample space Ω={1,…,n} and that for all i∈Ω, X2(i)=Xi,⋅T and X1(i)=Yi : C22 would be 1nXTX, and C21 would be 1n(XTY), so that we get β^=(1nXTX)−1(1nXTY)=C22−1C21, and so X^1=(C12C22−1)X2 translates into the well known formula X^1=β^TX2.↩︎

  5. This presentation of the VSS criterion comes from the lectures of one its authors, W.Revelle (who is also the author of the psych R package we are using here).↩︎
